perturbation.original_text: the confidentiality of such information is maintained; b) it will not use the information for any commercial purpose or manufacture without obtaining a written licence or other agreement from the disclosing party; c) it will not disclose the information to any third party without written permission; d) it will not disclose the information to employ ees other than those above except to the extent necessary to fulfil the purposes set out above and all such other employees to which it will disclose it will be made aware of the confidential nature of the information, and the conditions of disclosure herein; e) it will not make any copy of or abstract of the information without specific written permission from the disclosing party; f) it will acknowledge the source (i.e. one of the organisations signatory to this Agreement) of, and will mark "Confidential", any drawing, document or software incorporating the information.
perturbation.changed_text: the confidentiality of such information is maintained to a reasonable extent; b) it will aim to not use the information for any commercial purpose or manufacture without obtaining a written licence or other agreement from the disclosing party; c) it will aim to not disclose the information to any third party without written permission; d) it will disclose the information to employ ees at its discretion; e) it will make copies of or abstracts of the information as needed; f) it will acknowledge the source (i.e. one of the organisations signatory to this Agreement) of, any drawing, document or software incorporating the information.
perturbation.explanation: Several key obligations have been weakened: 'will' replaced by 'aim to,' introducing a lower standard of commitment. The removal of 'except to the extent necessary' from the employee disclosure clause gives the receiving party free reign to disclose it to whomever. Removing the need for 'written permission' to copy or abstract the information and now states it can do so 'as needed' also creates a contradiction against the purpose of the agreement of maintaining and protecting confidential information. By changing the level of discretion, now employees can make copies when needed and disclose the information at its own discretion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The receiving party will receive all confidential in formation (whether recorded in writing or by other means or given orally without record) which is discl osed in connection with this Agreement subject to the following conditions: a) it will take all proper and reasonable measures to ensure that the confidentiality of such information is maintained to a reasonable extent; b) it will aim to not use the information for any commercial purpose or manufacture without obtaining a written licence or other agreement from the disclosing party; c) it will aim to not disclose the information to any third party without written permission; d) it will disclose the information to employ ees at its discretion; e) it will make copies of or abstracts of the information as needed; f) it will acknowledge the source (i.e. one of the organisations signatory to this Agreement) of, any drawing, document or software incorporating the information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens obligations regarding confidentiality, use, disclosure, and copying, replacing firm commitments with weaker 'aim to' statements and granting discretionary power, contradicting the purpose of maintaining and protecting confidential information as per the original agreement. Specifically, the changes in clauses b, c, d and e, directly conflict with the overall intention and reasonable expectation of strict confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: PROVIDED that the obligations herein undertaken will not apply to: a) information which at the time of disclosure is in the public domain or which after disclosure becomes part of the public domain through no fault of the recipient, or b) information which the recipient party can show was in its possession at the time of disclosure or which is independently developed by the recipient and wa s not acquired directly or indirectly from the disclosing party, or c) information which is made public at any time by the disclosing party, or by others with the permission of the disclosing party, or d) information which is received by the receivi ng party from a third party without similar restriction and without breach of this Agreement. e) information which is required to be disclosed by legal process, law or regulatory authority.
perturbation.changed_text: PROVIDED that the obligations herein undertaken will not apply to: a) information which is generally known, or b) information which the recipient party possesses, or c) information disclosed by the disclosing party, or d) information received from a third party. e) information required by law.
perturbation.explanation: The clauses have been significantly broadened. 'Information which at the time of disclosure is in the public domain' is changed to 'information which is generally known.' This is much wider and easier to argue. Similarly, removing 'without similar restriction' from the third-party disclosure clause means any information received from a third party is exempt, irrespective of confidentiality obligations. This severely limits the scope of what's protected and contradicts the purpose of the agreement. By allowing a more open sharing of the information received, it contradicts the core principles and intentions of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Under the terms of this Agreement there is no explic it or implied transfer of ownership to the receiving party of any drawings, documents or softwar e, or the copyright subsisting in them. PROVIDED that the obligations herein undertaken will not apply to: a) information which is generally known, or b) information which the recipient party possesses, or c) information disclosed by the disclosing party, or d) information received from a third party. e) information required by law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly broadens the exceptions to confidentiality, stating that the obligations do not apply to 'information which is generally known' or 'information received from a third party' without restriction. This directly contradicts the contradicted text's implied restriction on the transfer of ownership and copyright, as the receiving party can now freely use and potentially distribute information received from third parties, even if it contains drawings, documents, or software protected by copyright, undermining the intended protection.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree that at all times, during and after the current discussions, and thereafter for a period of ten (10) years, starting from the effective date of this Agreement, not to communicate or to divulge to third parties confidential information received from the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree not to communicate or to divulge to third parties confidential information received from the other party during discussions.
perturbation.explanation: The clause limiting disclosure is reduced to apply only 'during discussions,' removing the post-discussion confidentiality requirement and the ten-year limitation, drastically reducing the agreement's protective effect and undermining its initial intent. This directly contradicts any interpretation of protecting the disclosing party's interests beyond the immediate discussion period. The explicit reduction of time makes a clear contradiction with the initial agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Both parties agree not to communicate or to divulge to third parties confidential information received from the other party during discussions.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated confidentiality for ten years post-agreement, while the changed text limits it to 'during discussions'. This creates a direct conflict with the contradicted text, which reflects only the 'during discussions' limitation. Because the original text and changed text have fundamental differences in the extent to which they apply confidentiality, the original text contradicts the changed text, and therefore a contradiction exists.
